text: A sixty-nine-year-old female presenting an adenocarcinoma, five point five centimeters away from the anal margin, underwent neoadjuvant chemoradiation and the preoperative stage was reduced to T two N zero M zero.
A reusable D port is inserted into the anus, providing access for the surgical procedure.
The video shows the tumor located 5.5 centimeters from the anal margin, with visible anatomical landmarks indicating the tumor's position relative to the anus.
A butterfly needle is inserted intraluminally, and indocyanine green (ICG) injections are performed peritumorally at the nine, twelve, and three o'clock positions around the tumor.
The surgeon performs two injections into the rectal mucosa and directly into the rectal tumor.
The surgeon removes the butterfly needle from the rectal mucosa after completing the ICG injections around the tumor.
The laparoscopic view reveals ICG fluorescence diffusing around the rectum and along the lymphatic vessels, highlighting the anatomical structures and pathways involved.
Using abdominal laparoscopy, the surgeon performs vascular dissection around the rectum, focusing on the blood vessels associated with the rectal tumor.

Monocurved reusable instruments are inserted into the transanal D-port, initiating transanal minimally invasive surgery with the placement of a purse-string suture one centimeter below the rectal tumor.
The surgeon performs a suture using submucosal bites with PDS-II zero suture material, securing the tissue around the rectal tumor.
The surgeon performs an extracorporeal knot to close the lumen, visibly pushing the tissue to ensure closure at the end of the procedure.
A monocurved coagulating hook is inserted through the transanal D-port, and the surgeon performs a mucosal dissection delimitation using a tattoo technique.
The surgeon initiates a perforation through the fourth thickness of the rectal wall, beginning posteriorly.
The pneumo-retrorectum technique enhances the dissected space, allowing the indocyanine green (ICG) dye to become visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the rectal wall using monocurved grasping forceps and a monocurve coagulating hook, progressing from posterior to lateral and anterior directions.
The surgeon uses the presence of ICG to reveal and dissect the lower part of the recto-sacral ligament, continuing until the pre-sacral fascia is visually identified.
The surgeon carefully frees the presacral fascia from the visceral rectal fascia, maintaining the presacral fascia in an inferior position.
Fluorescence imaging reveals the presence of ICG above the dissected plane, highlighting the anatomical structures and aiding in surgical navigation.
The surgeon continues the dissection, liberating the mesorectum from the presacral fascia, with visible separation of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly and laterally on both sides, using monocurved grasping forceps and a monocurve coagulating hook to separate the mesorectum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon was able to work in good ergonomic conditions thanks to the curves of the instruments.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection, freeing the rectum from the vagina within the rectovaginal septum, carefully separating these structures.
Fluorescence imaging reveals the presence of ICG above the dissected plane, while the surgeon gently pushes the vagina anteriorly to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, carefully avoiding injury to the splanchnic nerves while separating the mesorectum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates the right inferior rectal artery, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the artery, completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the anterior dissection of the rectum, progressing until the abdominal scope becomes visible, indicating the advancement of the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection at the level of the promontory, marking the end of this phase of the procedure.
The surgeon maintains the rectum and surrounding periadipous tissue in a downward position, utilizing the fluorescent plane of dissection for guidance.
The surgeon perforates the peritoneal sheet anteriorly, advancing the dissection towards Douglas' pouch.
The surgeon performs perforation at the level of the promontory and laterally on both sides, continuing the dissection process.
The final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is displayed, showing the completed dissection with clear anatomical planes and structures visible.
A plastic protection is inserted into the D-port, and the rectum is placed inside the protective sheath.
The D-port is removed while the specimen is securely held with a monocurve grasper.
The surgeon removes the specimen transanally, ensuring the orifice is protected with a plastic sheath during extraction.
The surgeon cuts the descending colon and introduces a circular anvil into the colic lumen, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon pushes the circular anvil into the pelvis and repositions the D-port, preparing for the next stage of the procedure.
The surgeon closes the rectal stump using a PDS-II-Zero running suture, securing the tissue with precise stitching.
Under transanal and laparoscopic scope control, the surgeon performs a purse-string suture, placing four thickness bites to secure the tissue.
Thanks to the curves of the instruments, the surgeon continued to work under satisfying ergonomic conditions.
The monocurved anvil grasping forceps hold the spike of the anvil centrally within the rectal stump as the surgeon completes the extracorporeal knot at the end of the suture.
The rectal stump is closed around the spike of the anvil, securing it in place.
The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the D-port from the surgical site, completing the closure of the rectal stump.
The surgeon inserts the circular stapler into the rectum and connects it to the anvil positioned within the rectal stump, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon removes the monocurved anvil grasper and performs the anastomosis under abdominal laparoscopic control, ensuring the connection between the rectal stump and the anvil.
The video displays a final endoluminal view of the anastomosis, showcasing the internal appearance of the newly formed connection within the rectal lumen.
